the-wall: A massive fortification of ice, stone, and ancient magic stretching along the northern border of the Seven Kingdoms, manned by the Night's Watch. Built after the Long Night to guard the realms of men against wildlings and the threat beyond, it has stood for thousands of years as one of the largest man-made structures in the known world.
city-kings-landing: The capital of the Seven Kingdoms, founded by Aegon the Conqueror at the site where his fleet landed before the Conquest of Westeros. Dominated by the Red Keep, it is the seat of the Iron Throne and the center of political power in the realm, as well as its largest and most populous city.
castle-hayford-castle: The seat of House Hayford, a minor noble house of the Crownlands less than a day's ride from King's Landing, sworn directly to the Iron Throne. During the First Blackfyre Rebellion, its lord briefly served as Hand of the King to Daeron II Targaryen, and the house had earlier supported Rhaenyra Targaryen's claim during the Dance of the Dragons.
castle-rosby: The seat of House Rosby, a minor noble house of the Crownlands just northeast of King's Landing, sworn directly to the Iron Throne. Their blazon is three red chevronels on ermine.
settlement-brindlewood: A small village in the Crownlands, along the Kingsroad near the border with the Riverlands.
castle-sows-horn: A small stone towerhouse in the Crownlands, seat of House Hogg, notable for walls unusually thick for its size. It stands close to the lands of House Wode, on ground sworn to House Hayford, near the border with the Riverlands.
town-tumbleton: A market town in the Reach, seat of House Footly, lying near the region's border with the Crownlands. During the Dance of the Dragons, the town was infamously destroyed by dragonfire in the Treasons of Tumbleton, when two turncloak dragonriders burned the settlement even as its defenders believed the battle already won.
ruin-high-heart: A hill sacred to the children of the forest in the Riverlands, ringed by the stumps of thirty-one weirwoods cut down by the Andals in ancient times. It is said to be haunted by the ghosts of the children who died there, and is home to the woods witch known as the ghost of High Heart, a diminutive seer who dreams of things to come.
town-duskendale: A port town on Blackwater Bay in the Crownlands, seat of House Rykker following the extinction of House Darklyn. It is best remembered for the Defiance of Duskendale, when Lord Denys Darklyn held King Aerys II Targaryen captive within the town for months, an event that destroyed his house and left a lasting mark on the king's sanity.
castle-antlers: A castle in the Crownlands, southwest of Maidenpool near the border with the Riverlands, seat of the minor noble House Buckwell.
castle-stokeworth: The seat of House Stokeworth, a minor noble house of the Crownlands sworn directly to King's Landing.
castle-harrenhal: The largest castle in the Seven Kingdoms, raised over forty years on the shore of the Gods Eye by Harren the Black at the cost of thousands of laborers' lives. Aegon the Conqueror burned Harren and his line within its walls using dragonfire, and the ruined, cavernous castle has since gained a reputation for cursing nearly every house to hold it.
other-rushing-falls: A site near the Gods Eye in the Riverlands.
town-tumblers-falls: A town in the southern Riverlands, situated on the Blackwater Rush.
town-stoney-sept: A walled market town in the southern Riverlands, built around a fountain shaped like a leaping trout and known for its many bells. It is best remembered as the site of the Battle of the Bells during Robert's Rebellion, where the wounded Robert Baratheon evaded capture by loyalist forces until relieved by Eddard Stark and Hoster Tully.
other-hollow-hill: A hidden cave in the Riverlands, its walls and tunnels threaded with the roots of ancient weirwoods. During the War of the Five Kings, it served as a secret refuge for outlaws and smallfolk fleeing the fighting that ravaged the region.
castle-acorn-hall: The seat of House Smallwood in the Riverlands, a small castle of stone curtain walls and an oak keep southeast of Riverrun. Once sworn directly to House Tully, the Smallwoods fought in several wars across Targaryen history, including the Dance of the Dragons, and are now vassals of House Vance.
castle-pinkmaiden: The seat of House Piper on the Red Fork near the border with the Westerlands. Loyal bannermen of House Tully, the Pipers fought on opposing sides of succession crises across Targaryen history, backing Prince Aegon under Maegor I and the Blacks during the Dance of the Dragons.
other-mummers-ford: A crossing of the Red Fork of the Trident in the western Riverlands. It was the site of a clash between Lannister forces and a company sent by Eddard Stark early in the War of the Five Kings, whose survivors would go on to form the brotherhood without banners.
ruin-sherrer: A small village in the Riverlands, one of several burned by raiders from the Westerlands early in the War of the Five Kings.
ruin-wendish-town: A small village in the Riverlands whose holdfast and inhabitants were destroyed by raiders from the Westerlands early in the War of the Five Kings.
settlement-sallydance: A small village on the Red Fork of the Trident in the Riverlands, its sept notable for its windows of leaded glass. During the Dance of the Dragons, it was attacked by Prince Aemond Targaryen and his dragon Vhagar.
settlement-lady-of-the-leaves: A hidden village built in the trees of the Riverlands, named for its reclusive elderly leader. It lies near the road to Hayford in the Crownlands, several days' travel from Lord Lychester's keep.
castle-lord-lychesters-keep: The seat of House Lychester in the Riverlands, a small square keep that has fallen into disrepair over the years. During the uprising of the Faith Militant, Queen Visenya Targaryen and her dragon Vhagar burned the seats of House Lychester and several of its neighbors.
castle-riverrun: The ancestral seat of House Tully at the confluence of the Tumblestone and the Red Fork in the western Riverlands. First among the river lords to join Aegon the Conqueror against King Harren Hoare, the Tullys were raised to Lords Paramount of the Trident, and the well-defended castle has rarely fallen to siege since.
castle-raventree-hall: The seat of House Blackwood in the Blackwood Vale of the Riverlands, an ancient castle whose godswood holds a colossal dead weirwood the Blackwoods claim was poisoned by their rivals, House Bracken. The Blackwoods once ruled the Riverlands as kings during the Age of Heroes before being driven south into the Riverlands by the Kings of Winter.
castle-darry: The seat of House Darry, once one of the most powerful houses of the Trident before Robert's Rebellion. Steadfast Targaryen loyalists, the Darrys lost much of their standing after backing the losing side of the rebellion, and their lands once stretched as far as the inn at the crossroads.
other-inn-of-the-kneeling-man: An inn on the Red Fork of the Trident in the Riverlands, named for the spot where Torrhen Stark, King in the North, is said to have knelt before Aegon the Conqueror. Its sign depicts the kneeling king in submission.
town-lord-harroways-town: A riverside town in the Riverlands where the three forks of the Trident converge, seat of House Roote though named for the extinct House Harroway. Travelers cross the river there by ferry, aboard a flat-bottomed boat known as Old King Andahar's two-headed water horse.
other-crossroads-inn: Stands where the Kingsroad, the Riverroad, and the High Road to the Vale all meet in the Riverlands, on lands once held by House Darry. First raised during the reign of King Jaehaerys I Targaryen and rebuilt many times since under different names, it remains one of the best-known waystations in the region.
town-saltpans: A town in the Riverlands, on the Bay of Crabs at the mouth of the Trident, seat of House Cox, its economy built on fishing and trading salt across the narrow sea.
other-quiet-isle: An island sanctuary at the mouth of the Trident in the Riverlands, home to a septry where male penitents go to atone for their sins under a vow of near-total silence. It is led by the Elder Brother, who tends the isle's ancient hermit's cave and permits only himself and his proctors to speak, and then only rarely.
castle-wickenden: The seat of House Waxley in the Vale of Arryn, a small town on the northern shore of the Bay of Crabs known throughout the Vale for its scented beeswax candles.
town-maidenpool: A walled port town on the Bay of Crabs in the eastern Riverlands, seat of House Mooton. Despite being considerably wealthier than their liege lords, House Tully, the Mootons were defeated and brought into the realm during Aegon's Conquest, after the Storm Kings and Ironborn kings had ruled the town in earlier centuries.
castle-rooks-rest: A castle on the northern shore of Blackwater Bay in the Crownlands, seat of House Staunton. During the Dance of the Dragons, it was the site of a battle in which Rhaenys Targaryen and her dragon Meleys, arriving to relieve the besieged castle, were killed by the combined attack of Aegon II and Aemond Targaryen on their dragons Sunfyre and Vhagar.
castle-dyre-den: A small castle of three crooked towers on a cliff above Crackclaw Point in the Crownlands, seat of House Brune of the Dyre Den. It is reached only by a steep, narrow cleft cut into the cliff face below the walls.
ruin-the-whispers: A ruined castle of House Crabb on Crackclaw Point in the Crownlands, long abandoned and overgrown with forest. Legend holds it takes its name from the whispering, disembodied heads of victims left by the fabled knight Ser Clarence Crabb, and in the novels it serves as a smugglers' cove where Brienne of Tarth confronts members of the Brave Companions.
castle-dragonstone: An island castle in Blackwater Bay, shaped by the Valyrians in the likeness of dragons and settled by House Targaryen more than a century before the Doom of Valyria. It served as Aegon the Conqueror's staging ground for the conquest of Westeros and, afterward, as the traditional seat of the heir to the Iron Throne, the Prince of Dragonstone, until Stannis Baratheon seized it during Robert's Rebellion.
castle-sharp-point: A castle at the northern end of Massey's Hook in the Crownlands, seat of House Bar Emmon since its founding by the Andal warlord Togarion Bar Emmon. The house broke from its liege the Storm King to support Aegon Targaryen during the Conquest, and Sharp Point has been sworn to Dragonstone ever since.
castle-stonedance: A castle on the eastern shore of Massey's Hook in the Crownlands, seat of the ancient House Massey, said to descend from the First Men. Lord Triston Massey abandoned his liege the Storm King to support Aegon the Conqueror and was rewarded with a seat as the first master of laws on Aegon's small council.
castle-haystack-hall: The seat of House Errol in the Stormlands, east of the kingsroad and south of the kingswood. During Aegon's Conquest, the Errols joined Houses Buckler and Fell in ambushing Orys Baratheon's host at the Wendwater before being driven into the kingswood by Rhaenys Targaryen and her dragon Meraxes.
castle-bronzegate: The seat of House Buckler on the kingsroad between the Wendwater and Storm's End. It was the site of one of House Durrandon's greatest victories, when King Monfryd V Durrandon defeated an alliance of seven Andal warlords there at the cost of his own life.
castle-felwood: The seat of House Fell at the southern edge of the kingswood, northwest of Storm's End. During Robert's Rebellion, Lord Fell remained loyal to the Iron Throne and was slain in single combat by Robert Baratheon at the Battles of Summerhall.
castle-storms-end: The seat of House Baratheon and one of the strongest castles in the Seven Kingdoms, built at Durran's Point on Shipbreaker Bay by the legendary Durran Godsgrief. It was the ancient seat of the Storm Kings of House Durrandon, and during Robert's Rebellion it withstood a year-long siege under Stannis Baratheon before being resupplied by the smuggler Davos Seaworth.
castle-griffins-roost: The seat of House Connington, a small but strong castle on a crag above Shipbreaker Bay in northwestern Cape Wrath. Fifty generations of Conningtons ruled from its Griffin Seat before Lord Jon Connington's disgrace during Robert's Rebellion cost the house most of its lands and standing.
castle-rain-house: The seat of House Wylde in the eastern Rainwood, on the southern shore of Shipbreaker Bay near the tip of Cape Wrath. Its lord, Jasper Wylde, served as master of laws under Kings Viserys I and Aegon II before being executed as a traitor when Rhaenyra Targaryen took King's Landing during the Dance of the Dragons.
castle-greenstone: The seat of House Estermont on the small, damp island of Estermont east of Cape Wrath, added to the realm of the Storm Kings under Erich the Sailmaker. Lady Cassana Estermont of Greenstone wed Lord Steffon Baratheon of Storm's End and bore him three sons: Robert, Stannis, and Renly.
castle-weeping-tower: Stands in the Weeping Town, a prosperous port of House Whitehead on the Stormlands' Sea of Dorne coast. The town takes its mournful name from the grief its people showed when the corpse of King Daeron I Targaryen was carried home from Dorne in 161 AC.
castle-mistwood: Mistfall is the seat of House Mertyns in the southern Rainwood of Cape Wrath, its lord's solar set high in the castle's Tower of Owls. During the First Dornish War, Lord Mertyns and his entire household were poisoned by a cask of wine.
castle-stonehelm: The ancient seat of House Swann in the Red Watch of the eastern Dornish Marches, built on Cape Wrath to control the Slayne, a river route that has brought the Swanns wealth and power for generations.
castle-crows-nest: The seat of House Morrigen, set in the mountains between Griffin's Roost and Stonehelm along the western edge of Cape Wrath and the Rainwood. During Aegon's Conquest, Lord Morrigen and the Bastard of Blackhaven led the vanguard of King Argilac Durrandon at the Last Storm, both slain by Rhaenys Targaryen's dragon Meraxes.
town-grandview: The seat of House Grandison in the Stormlands. During Robert's Rebellion, Lord Grandison at first plotted against Robert Baratheon alongside Houses Fell and Cafferen, but after their defeat at the Battles of Summerhall he became Robert's ally and later died of wounds suffered at the Battle of the Trident.
ruin-summerhall: A ruined castle in the Stormlands near the border with the Reach, once a lightly fortified Targaryen summer residence built in the Dornish Marches during the reign of King Daeron II. It burned in a mysterious fire in 259 AC that killed King Aegon V Targaryen, his son Prince Duncan, and the Lord Commander of the Kingsguard, Ser Duncan the Tall, the same day Prince Rhaegar Targaryen was born amid the tragedy.
castle-blackhaven: The seat of House Dondarrion, marcher lords who guard a pass through the Dornish Marches near the Boneway. According to legend, the first Dondarrion was raised to lordship by the Storm King after a bolt of purple lightning struck down two Dornish raiders who had ambushed him, an event echoed in the house's lightning-bolt sigil.
town-fawnton: A town in the Stormlands, seat of House Cafferen, near the border with the Reach.
castle-grassy-vale: A town in the Reach controlled by House Meadows from their castle, Grassfield Keep, on the northern shore of the Blueburn near the border with the Stormlands. During the Faith Militant uprising, Lord Meadows led a Reach host to crush the Poor Fellows of Wat the Hewer, though the fighting claimed his own life as well.
castle-wyl: The seat of House Wyl of the Boneway in northern Dorne, at the mouth of the River Wyl on the Sea of Dorne, its castle honeycombed with defensive tunnels and caverns. During the First Dornish War, a Lord Wyl committed one of the conflict's most infamous atrocities, and later Wyl lords held Prince Aemon the Dragonknight captive after the death of King Daeron I Targaryen under a peace banner.
castle-ghaston-grey: A bleak, crumbling castle-prison on a rocky island in the Sea of Dorne, used by House Martell to hold the vilest of criminals and traitors, left there to waste away.
castle-yronwood: The seat of House Yronwood, the most powerful house in Dorne after the ruling Martells, built in the foothills of the Red Mountains to guard the Stone Way. Once the seat of the Kings of Yronwood, who ruled half of Dorne and claimed the title High King before the coming of the Rhoynar, the house submitted to House Martell during Nymeria's War but retains the ancestral title of Bloodroyal.
castle-vultures-roost: A ruined castle in northern Dorne, standing near the source of the River Wyl deep in the Red Mountains.
castle-nightsong: The seat of House Caron, marcher lords of the Stormlands whose nightingale banner has flown over a thousand battlefields; the castle itself has been besieged no fewer than thirty-seven times in the last thousand years. During the First Dornish War, Lord Fowler burned Nightsong and carried off Lord Caron and his family as captives.
castle-ashford: The seat of House Ashford in the Reach, best known for hosting the tourney at Ashford Meadow in 209 AC. Held to celebrate a lord's daughter's name day, the tourney grew famous for an altercation between Ser Duncan the Tall and Prince Aerion Targaryen that led to the first trial of seven fought in over a century.
castle-cider-hall: The seat of House Fossoway of Cider Hall, a house of landed knights sworn to House Tyrell, standing near the confluence of the Cockleswhent and the Mander. Known as the red-apple Fossoways to distinguish them from their green-apple cousins of New Barrel, they claim descent from Foss the Archer, a son of the legendary Garth Greenhand.
castle-highgarden: The regional capital of the Reach, a castle of three concentric white stone walls on the Mander held for centuries by House Gardener as the Kings of the Reach. When Aegon the Conqueror slew King Mern IX Gardener at the Field of Fire, the castle's steward Harlan Tyrell surrendered it without a fight and was granted the seat and lordship of the Reach by Aegon.
castle-horn-hill: The seat of House Tarly, marcher lords of the Dornish Marches, said to have been built by legendary twin sons of Garth Greenhand atop its namesake hill in the wooded foothills of the Red Mountains. The Tarlys are keepers of Heartsbane, an ancestral Valyrian steel greatsword.
castle-brightwater-keep: The seat of House Florent near the source of the Honeywine in the Reach. The Florents claim a stronger blood tie to the extinct Kings of the Reach than the Tyrells, yet were passed over for the lordship of Highgarden when Aegon the Conqueror granted it to House Tyrell instead.
castle-bandallon: The seat of House Blackbar in the Reach, visited by King Jaehaerys I Targaryen during one of his royal progresses through the region.
castle-honeyholt: The seat of House Beesbury, a minor house of the Reach sworn to the Hightowers, standing along the Honeywine between Brightwater Keep and Oldtown. The Beesburys claim descent from Ellyn Ever Sweet, a daughter of the legendary Garth Greenhand.
city-oldtown: The oldest and among the largest cities in Westeros, built by the First Men around the Hightower, the seat of House Hightower on Battle Isle. It is home to the Citadel, the seat of the order of maesters, and to the Starry Sept, once the seat of the Faith of the Seven before the High Septon's move to King's Landing after Aegon's Conquest.
castle-blackcrown: The seat of House Bulwer on the northern shore of the Whispering Sound in the southwestern Reach, named for the singing cliffs of wind-carved stone that rise above its walls.
castle-three-towers: The seat of House Costayne, sworn to the Hightowers, standing on a cliff where the Whispering Sound meets the Redwyne Straits.
castle-uplands: A castle in the Reach, seat of House Mullendore, located along a river that flows west to the Honeywine near the border with Dorne.
castle-sunflower-hall: Sunhouse is the seat of House Cuy on the southern coast of the Reach, east of the Redwyne Straits and southeast of Oldtown. The Cuys trace their ancestry to Andal adventurers granted lands by the Three Sage Kings of House Gardener.
castle-blackmont: The seat of House Blackmont on the banks of the Torentine in the Red Mountains of Dorne. King Benedict Blackmont, said to transform himself into a giant vulture, was defeated during Nymeria's War and sent to the Wall along with five other Dornish kings.
castle-high-hermitage: The seat of House Dayne of High Hermitage, a lesser branch and bannermen of House Dayne of Starfall, standing in the Red Mountains northeast of Starfall.
castle-starfall: The seat of House Dayne, said to have been built where the first Dayne found a fallen star while following its path across the sky. Once the seat of the Kings of the Torrentine, the Daynes are keepers of Dawn, an ancestral greatsword said to be forged from the star's heart and wielded by the Sword of the Morning.
castle-sandstone: The seat of House Qorgyle, raised by Andal adventurers around the only well for fifty leagues in the western dunes of Dorne. Prince Oberyn Martell was fostered here as a child.
castle-skyreach: The seat of House Fowler, its soaring stone towers carved into the cliffs above the Prince's Pass. Once rulers of the pass as the Kings of Stone and Sky, the Fowlers submitted to House Martell during Nymeria's War but retained their lordship as Wardens of the Prince's Pass.
castle-kingsgrave: The seat of House Manwoody, standing roughly halfway through the Prince's Pass in the Red Mountains of Dorne. It takes its name from the house's founder, who is said to have slain a king.
other-tower-of-joy: A round tower at the northern mouth of the Prince's Pass in the Red Mountains of Dorne, between Kingsgrave and Nightsong. At the end of Robert's Rebellion, Eddard Stark and six companions fought three knights of the Kingsguard there; only Eddard and Howland Reed survived, and Eddard later had the tower torn down to raise cairns for the fallen.
castle-hellholt: The seat of House Uller, built around a well in the heart of the Dornish desert and named for an incident in which the Ullers locked their rivals inside and burned them alive. It was the site of the death of Rhaenys Targaryen and her dragon Meraxes, struck down by a scorpion bolt during the First Dornish War.
castle-vaith: The seat of House Vaith, a tall pale castle on the river of the same name in the hill country east of Dorne's deep dunes.
castle-salt-shore: The seat of House Gargalen on Dorne's southern coast along the Summer Sea. Prince Doran Martell squired here as a boy under Lord Gargalen.
castle-godsgrace: The seat of House Allyrion, standing near where the Vaith and the Scourge meet to form the Greenblood. Once rulers of their own petty kingdom during the Age of the Hundred Kingdoms, the pious Allyrions were briefly overlords of House Martell before Nymeria's War reversed their fortunes.
castle-the-tor: The seat of House Jordayne on the southern coast of the Sea of Dorne, once the seat of kings to whom House Martell itself paid homage before Nymeria's War overturned Dorne's old order.
castle-ghost-hill: The seat of House Toland on the southern shore of the Sea of Dorne near the Broken Arm. Its banner, a green dragon biting its own tail, commemorates a Toland fool who died in his lord's place before Aegon the Conqueror during the First Dornish War.
castle-lemonwood: The seat of House Dalt near the mouth of the Greenblood in Dorne, close to Planky Town. The site once held a wooden motte-and-bailey castle belonging to early Dornish river kings before their river kingdom splintered.
castle-sunspear: The Old Palace is the seat of House Martell and the capital of Dorne, built on the Broken Arm and surrounded on three sides by the sea. Its towers, raised in Rhoynish fashion after the union of the Martells with the warrior queen Nymeria, were among the few Dornish strongholds the Targaryens never dared burn during their wars of conquest.
town-planky-town: The largest settlement in Dorne, a trading town built entirely of lashed-together boats and planked walkways at the mouth of the Greenblood. Founded by river-dwelling orphans as a gathering place to trade with foreign ships, it was burned to the waterline by Rhaenys Targaryen and her dragon Meraxes at the outbreak of the First Dornish War.
castle-water-gardens: A palace and private retreat of House Martell on the coast west of Sunspear, raised by Prince Maron Martell as a wedding gift for his bride, Princess Daenerys Targaryen, to mark Dorne's union with the Seven Kingdoms. Its pools have long been opened to the children of highborn and smallfolk alike.
ruin-shandystone: An abandoned holdfast in southeastern Dorne, its ruins of fluted pillars, triple arches, and sand-scoured statues left empty after its well ran dry around 200 AC.
town-tall-trees-town: The capital of the Summer Isles, a port town on the southwestern coast of the island of Walano shaded by the immense Talking Trees, whose trunks are carved with centuries of Summer Islander history. In 19 AC, pirates sacked the town and carried off thousands into slavery, an atrocity that prompted King Aegon I Targaryen to order the raising of walls around King's Landing.
castle-longtable: The seat of House Merryweather at the confluence of the Mander and the Blueburn in the Reach. Once a prominent Reach family, the Merryweathers lost much of their wealth and standing after Robert's Rebellion, from which they have never fully recovered.
castle-bitterbridge: Bitterbridge, once called Stonebridge, is the seat of House Caswell where the Mander meets the roseroad in the Reach, its name commemorating a battle so bloody during the Faith Militant uprising that the river ran red for twenty leagues.
castle-goldengrove: The seat of House Rowan in the northern Reach, east of Red Lake, added to the Kingdom of the Reach through a pact of friendship and mutual defense with Garth III Gardener. The Rowans fought and died alongside the Gardeners at the Field of Fire during Aegon's Conquest.
castle-red-lake: The seat of House Crane on the northeastern shore of its namesake lake in the northwestern Reach, near the border with the Westerlands, joined to the Kingdom of the Reach through a pact of friendship and mutual defense with Garth III Gardener.
castle-old-oak: The seat of House Oakheart, an old and powerful Reach family that claims descent from a legendary son of Garth Greenhand. Despite their line predating House Tyrell's, the Oakhearts fought and died beside the Gardeners at the Field of Fire and became bannermen to the new lords of Highgarden.
castle-crakehall: The seat of House Crakehall, one of the principal bannermen of House Lannister of Casterly Rock, standing along the Ocean Road between the Sunset Sea and a great forest. Aegon the Conqueror visited the castle on several of his royal progresses, and it later served as a training ground for young squires of the Westerlands, including a young Jaime Lannister.
castle-cornfield: The seat of House Swyft, a knightly house sworn to Casterly Rock, standing southeast of the Rock between Crakehall and Red Lake.
castle-silverhill: The seat of House Serrett in the hilly country of the Westerlands, formed by the union of First Men and Andal nobles during the coming of the Andals. It is one of the principal houses sworn to House Lannister of Casterly Rock.
castle-clegane-hall: The seat of House Clegane, a landed knightly house southeast of Lannisport. Its founder, a kennelmaster at Casterly Rock, was granted the towerhouse and lands after losing a leg saving Lord Tytos Lannister from a lioness, and the keep has since gained a grim reputation befitting its most famous lord, Ser Gregor Clegane.
ruin-tarbeck-hall: A ruined castle near Castamere in the Westerlands, once the seat of House Tarbeck. Rebuilt with Lannister gold after centuries of decline, it was destroyed by Tywin Lannister in 261 AC when the Tarbecks joined House Reyne in open rebellion against Casterly Rock.
city-lannisport: The largest city of the Westerlands, grown from a village a mile from Casterly Rock into a walled city with a thriving natural harbor. Ruled by a lesser branch of House Lannister, it prospered from trade and fishing while its parent branch at Casterly Rock rose to become Kings of the Rock and, later, Lords Paramount of the West.
castle-feastfires: The seat of House Prester at the southwestern tip of a Westerlands peninsula, south of Fair Isle. During the Dance of the Dragons, Ironborn longships ambushed a westermen fleet gathered beneath its walls, killing Lord Prester among others.
town-kayce: A market town and the seat of House Kenning of Kayce, on a peninsula of the Sunset Sea south of Fair Isle. According to legend it was won for House Lannister by the Ironborn turncoat Herrock the Whoreson, who signaled the town's whores to open a postern gate from within.
settlement-oxcross: A village in the Westerlands near Lannisport, within a few days' ride of Casterly Rock.
castle-sarsfield: The seat of House Sarsfield, an inland castle along the river road northeast of Casterly Rock and southwest of the Golden Tooth. Like several other Westerlands houses, the Sarsfields trace their origins to intermarriage between Andal settlers and the First Men Kings of the Rock.
castle-deep-den: The seat of House Lydden in the hilly eastern Westerlands, standing near the Goldroad between Hornvale and Silverhill.
castle-hornvale: The seat of House Brax in the hills of the eastern Westerlands, near the headwaters of the Red Fork south of the Golden Tooth. The Braxes are one of several Westerlands houses formed through intermarriage between Andal conquerors and the First Men Kings of the Rock.
castle-golden-tooth: A small but formidable castle of House Lefford, commanding the mountain pass between the Westerlands and the Riverlands. Any army seeking to invade the Westerlands from the east must ordinarily force the Tooth, making it one of the most strategically vital strongholds in the region.
castle-wayfarers-rest: A castle in the Riverlands, seat of House Vance, standing near the Red Fork not far from the border with the Westerlands.
ruin-castamere: A ruined, largely subterranean stronghold in the Westerlands, once the seat of the wealthy House Reyne and its rich mines of gold and silver. In 261 AC, Tywin Lannister crushed the Reyne-Tarbeck rebellion by damming a nearby stream and flooding the mine, drowning every Reyne within, an act of annihilation still remembered in the song "The Rains of Castamere."
other-pendric-hills: The site of a Westerlands gold mine, one of several that fund House Lannister's fortune.
castle-the-crag: The seat of House Westerling on the coast of the Sunset Sea in the northwestern Westerlands, an ancient First Men line whose fortunes have declined along with their failed mines, leaving the castle more ruin than stronghold.
castle-banefort: The seat of House Banefort on the coast of the Sunset Sea in the northwestern Westerlands, once ruled by the Hooded Kings of the First Men. The last of that line, Morgon Banefort, was defeated after twenty years of war by Loreon I Lannister, King of the Rock.
castle-ashemark: The seat of House Marbrand, standing in the hills of the Westerlands near the source of the Tumblestone. Formed through intermarriage during the Andal conquest, the Marbrands became closely tied to House Lannister when Jeyne Marbrand wed Lord Tytos Lannister of Casterly Rock.
other-nunns-deep: The site of a Westerlands gold mine, one of several that fund House Lannister's fortune.
other-stone-mill: A crossing of the Red Fork of the Trident in the western Riverlands, likely north of Pinkmaiden Castle. It was the site of heavy fighting during the early clashes of the War of the Five Kings.
castle-stone-hedge: The seat of House Bracken in the Riverlands, an old house whose lords once ruled as river kings during the Age of Heroes. Bitter and ancient rivals of neighboring House Blackwood of Raventree Hall, the Brackens are among the principal bannermen sworn to House Tully of Riverrun.
town-fairmarket: A market town on the Blue Fork of the Trident in the northern Riverlands. It was the site of a decisive battle in which Harwyn Hardhand of the Iron Islands defeated the Storm King Arrec Durrandon to seize control of the Riverlands, after which Harwyn's son Halleck Hoare made Fairmarket his seat.
ruin-oldstones: A ruined castle on a hill above the Blue Fork of the Trident in the Riverlands, once the seat of House Mudd and the River Kings. It was here that Tristifer IV Mudd, the Hammer of Justice, was defeated in his hundredth and final battle against the invading Andals, ending First Men rule over the Riverlands.
castle-the-bloody-gate: A fortified chokepoint where the high road narrows to a passage barely wide enough for four riders abreast, guarded by twin watchtowers joined by a covered stone bridge. Carved into the Mountains of the Moon at the entrance to the Vale of Arryn, it has broken every army that has tried to force it since the Age of Heroes.
castle-the-eyrie: The seat of House Arryn, a small but famously impregnable castle set high on the Giant's Lance in the Mountains of the Moon. It is known for the Moon Door, an opening in its High Hall through which condemned prisoners are cast to their deaths on the mountainside far below.
castle-strongsong: The seat of House Belmore in the Vale of Arryn, set among a series of lakes in the Mountains of the Moon. The Belmores are descended from the First Men and submitted to House Arryn after their ally Robar II Royce was defeated in the Battle of the Seven Stars.
castle-hearts-home: The seat of House Corbray, an old but impoverished house in the Vale of Arryn, standing where a glacial river meets a narrow bay of the narrow sea. The Corbrays are best known for Lady Forlorn, their ancestral Valyrian steel longsword.
castle-redfort: The ancient seat of House Redfort in the Vale of Arryn, descended from the First Men who once allied with Robar II Royce against the invading Andals. Like other Valemen who resisted the Andals, the Redforts ultimately submitted to House Arryn as Kings of Mountain and Vale.
castle-ironoaks: The seat of House Waynwood in the Vale of Arryn, standing beside a lake east of the Eyrie. During the coming of the Andals, King Robar II Royce won a battle beneath its walls against a First Men warlord known as the Hammer of the Hills.
castle-old-anchor: The seat of House Melcolm in the Vale of Arryn, standing where a river flows into the narrow sea east of Ironoaks.
castle-runestone: The seat of House Royce in the Vale of Arryn, ancient stronghold of the First Men Bronze Kings on the coast of the narrow sea. It houses a suit of rune-inscribed bronze armor thought to protect its wearer from harm, though many lords of Runestone have died wearing it all the same.
city-gulltown: The largest settlement in the Vale of Arryn, a port city with a sheltered natural harbor at the northern tip of the Bay of Crabs, ruled by House Grafton. Its position makes it a key gateway between King's Landing, Braavos, and the North, and its waters saw a mutually destructive naval battle between House Arryn and House Targaryen during Aegon's Conquest, as well as the opening battle of Robert's Rebellion.
castle-longbow-hall: The seat of House Hunter in the eastern Vale of Arryn. According to legend, the First Men lord of Longbow Hall pledged his house to Ser Robar II Royce after losing an archery contest to him during the Andal invasion.
castle-snakewood: The seat of House Lynderly, standing on the southernmost of the Fingers along the mountainous coast of the Vale of Arryn. The Fingers were the first Vale shores to see Andal longships, invited across the narrow sea by rival First Men petty kings vying for supremacy.
castle-baelish-keep: An old, unnamed flint tower on the smallest of the Fingers, ancestral seat of House Baelish, commanding no more than a few stony acres and a village of a dozen families. Its current lord, Petyr Baelish, mockingly nicknamed it the Drearfort, possibly echoing House Bolton's Dreadfort.
castle-coldwater: The seat of House Coldwater in the Vale of Arryn, standing near a river mouth west of the Fingers. Sworn to the Royces of Runestone, the Coldwaters are descended from the First Men who resisted, then submitted to, the Andal conquest of the Vale.
castle-seagard: The seat of House Mallister on the western coast of the Riverlands, a fortress built before Aegon's Conquest to guard against Ironborn raiders. During Greyjoy's Rebellion, Lord Jason Mallister repelled an Ironborn assault beneath its walls, ringing the castle's Booming Tower for only the second time in three hundred years.
castle-the-twins: A pair of identical fortified castles belonging to House Frey, joined by a bridge and tower spanning the Green Fork of the Trident. The crossing is one of the most strategically important in Westeros, and the Freys have never failed to collect their toll on it in the six hundred years they have held it.
castle-greywater-watch: The seat of House Reed, rulers of the crannogmen of the Neck in the North. Built upon a moving crannog in the swamps, it is said to never occupy the same spot twice, making it nearly impossible for enemies to find.
ruin-moat-cailin: One of the North's most important strongholds, a ruined castle that commands the causeway through the swamps of the Neck, the only safe passage for armies moving between the North and the Riverlands. Though only three of its original twenty towers still stand, its position amid treacherous bogs has let it hold back armies many times its own size throughout the ages.
town-sisterton: A mean, mud-streeted town on the isle of Sweetsister in the Bite, seat of House Borrell and known as a haven for smugglers. The Sistermen were once independent pirate-kings before bending the knee to the Eyrie in exchange for aid against a northern invasion, beginning centuries of Vale rule over the Three Sisters.
castle-oldcastle: The seat of House Locke on the northern shore of the Bite, southeast of White Harbor. Once the royal seat of the Lockes when they ruled as petty kings after the Long Night, the house was reduced to vassalage by the Kings of Winter and remains sworn to House Stark.
city-white-harbor: The largest city north of the Neck and the North's principal trade port, built at the mouth of the White Knife by House Manderly. The Manderlys were driven from their lands in the Reach roughly a thousand years ago and welcomed into the North as bannermen of House Stark, where they used their southron wealth to found and grow the city around their seat, the New Castle.
castle-ramsgate: A minor castle in the North at the mouth of the Broken Branch, between White Harbor and Widow's Watch. It falls within the sphere of influence of House Manderly of White Harbor.
castle-widows-watch: The seat of House Flint of Widow's Watch, probably the most powerful of the several Flint branches in the North. Located on a peninsula between the Shivering Sea and the Bite, its founders are said to descend from younger sons of the mountain Flints who left in search of land and wives.
castle-hornwood: The seat of House Hornwood, one of the principal bannermen sworn to House Stark in the North, bearing a brown bull moose as their sigil. Like other northern houses, its lords answered Robb Stark's call to arms during the War of the Five Kings, marching south with the northern host.
castle-the-dreadfort: The seat of House Bolton, a grim fortress of thick walls and jagged towers on the Weeping Water. Once the stronghold of the Red Kings who ruled the eastern North before the Starks' ascendancy, it is infamous for House Bolton's ancient practice of flaying enemies and displaying their skins within its halls.
castle-cerwyn: The seat of House Cerwyn, one of the Starks' closest and most loyal bannermen, located half a day's ride from Winterfell. The house has been tied to House Stark by marriage on several occasions across its history.
castle-winterfell: The ancestral seat of House Stark, said to have been built by Brandon the Builder in the Age of Heroes and ruled by the Starks as Kings of Winter for thousands of years. King Torrhen Stark yielded peacefully to Aegon the Conqueror, after which the Starks continued to rule the North as Lords of Winterfell and Wardens of the North.
castle-deepwood-motte: The seat of House Glover, an old wooden motte-and-bailey castle in the northern wolfswood east of Sea Dragon Point. Once the royal seat of the Glovers when they ruled as petty kings in the Age of Heroes, it was besieged and captured by Asha Greyjoy's Ironborn during the War of the Five Kings before being retaken by Stannis Baratheon's forces.
castle-last-hearth: The seat of House Umber, set in a forest north of Winterfell and the Dreadfort. Its lords led Umber levies south to join Robb Stark's war effort during the War of the Five Kings, leaving the castle in the hands of castellans in their absence.
castle-karhold: The seat of House Karstark, founded near the Grey Cliffs after Karlon Stark was granted lands for putting down a rebel lord. As a cadet branch of House Stark and one of its principal bannermen, the Karstarks have intermarried with the Starks of Winterfell repeatedly over the centuries.
ruin-queenscrown: An abandoned tower holdfast on a small lake island in the New Gift, its name commemorating a visit from Queen Alysanne Targaryen, who stopped there while flying to the Wall on her dragon Silverwing. Villagers painted the tower's merlons gold in her honor, though the settlement has long since been abandoned as the Gift's inhabitants fled south from wildling raids.
castle-castle-black: The headquarters of the Night's Watch and seat of its Lord Commander, standing along the Wall near the northern end of the Kingsroad. Unlike a true castle it has no walls of its own beyond the Wall itself, and it is one of only three of the Watch's original nineteen strongholds still garrisoned, alongside the Shadow Tower and Eastwatch-by-the-Sea.
settlement-moles-town: A settlement in the Gift a half league south of Castle Black, most of it built underground in warm cellars and tunnels to shelter from the cold. It is best known among the Night's Watch for its brothel, one of the few places brothers of the Watch can find female company.
castle-shadow-tower: The westernmost of the three castles still manned by the Night's Watch, standing where the Wall meets the northern mountains. It guards the western reaches of the Wall with a much smaller garrison than Castle Black, overlooking the abandoned ruins of Sentinel Stand and Westwatch-by-the-Bridge further west.
ruin-westwatch-by-the-bridge: The westernmost of the Night's Watch's abandoned castles, standing near the Bridge of Skulls east of the manned Shadow Tower. Little else is recorded of its history since its abandonment.
ruin-sentinel-stand: An abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between the manned Shadow Tower and the ruin of Greyguard.
ruin-greyguard: An abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Sentinel Stand and Stonedoor.
ruin-stonedoor: An abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Greyguard and Hoarfrost Hill.
ruin-hoarfrost-hill: An abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Stonedoor and Icemark.
ruin-icemark: An abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Hoarfrost Hill and the Nightfort.
ruin-nightfort: The oldest and largest of the Night's Watch's castles, twice the age of Castle Black, abandoned during the reign of King Jaehaerys I Targaryen when the Watch could no longer afford to maintain it. Its ruins hold the Black Gate, an ancient weirwood portal through the Wall, and dark legends including the reign of the Night's King and the tale of the Rat Cook.
ruin-deep-lake: An abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between the Nightfort and Queensgate.
ruin-queensgate: Queensgate, once known as Snowgate, is an abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Deep Lake and Castle Black.
ruin-oakenshield: An abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Castle Black and Woodswatch-by-the-Pool.
ruin-woodswatch-by-the-pool: An abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Oakenshield and Sable Hall.
ruin-sable-hall: An abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Woodswatch-by-the-Pool and Rimegate. In 50 AC, the knight tasked with restoring it, Ser Raymund Mallery, instead rebelled from the Watch in hopes of claiming the castle as his own seat.
ruin-rimegate: An abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Sable Hall and Long Barrow. In 50 AC, the knight tasked with restoring it, Ser Olyver Bracken, instead rebelled from the Watch in hopes of claiming the castle as his own seat.
ruin-long-barrow: An abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Rimegate and Torches.
ruin-torches: An abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Long Barrow and Greenguard.
ruin-greenguard: An abandoned castle of the Night's Watch along the Wall, lying between Torches and Eastwatch-by-the-Sea.
castle-eastwatch-by-the-sea: The easternmost of the three castles still manned by the Night's Watch, a windswept port on the Bay of Seals with the smallest garrison of the three. It serves as the Watch's link to the sea, receiving supplies and reinforcements that the order can no longer sustain across all nineteen of its ancient castles.
settlement-whitetree: A small free folk village in the haunted forest beyond the Wall, shaded by an immense weirwood tree with a trunk nearly eight feet wide. Its carved face bears signs suggesting the villagers practiced burning their dead within its hollow mouth.
castle-crasters-keep: A small free folk holdfast in the haunted forest beyond the Wall, home to Craster, a wildling who marries his own daughters and answers to no law but his own. Despite his unsavory reputation, Craster is regarded as a friend to the Night's Watch and has offered its rangers shelter beyond the Wall.
ruin-fist-of-the-first-men: A solitary, fist-shaped hill in the haunted forest beyond the Wall, crowned by an ancient ringfort raised by the First Men during the Dawn Age. The Night's Watch made camp within its ruined walls while searching for the missing wildling host during the great ranging beyond the Wall.
settlement-pennytree: A large, well-fortified village in the Riverlands, named for an ancient oak tree studded with copper pennies nailed into its trunk by travelers. It sits in a long-disputed area between the lands of House Bracken and House Blackwood, and though burned during the war, its stout holdfast let most of the village survive to be rebuilt.
city-lys: One of the nine Free Cities of Essos, a mercantile city built on a fertile island off the coast of the Disputed Lands. Founded as a Valyrian trade colony, it retains the strongest Valyrian bloodline of any Free City, with the pale skin, silver-gold hair, and light eyes of the old dragonlords still common among its people.
city-tyrosh: One of the nine Free Cities of Essos, founded as a Valyrian military outpost to guard the shipping lanes through the nearby Stepstones. Its wealth grew from a rare sea snail whose secretions produce a dye prized by Valyrian nobles, and after the Doom of Valyria it joined Lys and Myr to form the short-lived Triarchy, the so-called Kingdom of the Three Daughters.
city-myr: One of the nine Free Cities of Essos, founded by Valyrian merchant adventurers who conquered an earlier Andal town on the site. Ruled by a conclave of magisters, the city fell under Volantene rule for two generations during the Century of Blood before rebelling alongside Lys.
city-pentos: One of the nine Free Cities of Essos, ruled by a council of magisters and a largely ceremonial Prince of Pentos, who can be sacrificed to appease the gods if the city suffers famine or a lost war. Pentos has fought and lost several wars against Braavos over the practice of slavery.
ruin-ghoyan-drohe: A ruined Rhoynar city on the Little Rhoyne in the Velvet Hills of Essos, once a green and flowering place of canals and fountains before it was burned by Valyrian dragons during the Rhoynish Wars. Its broken temples and palaces have long since sunk into reeds and stagnant pools.
city-norvos: One of the nine Free Cities of Essos, founded by Valyrian religious dissenters who rejected the Freehold's tolerance of other faiths. The city is a theocracy ruled in truth by its secretive bearded priests, who select its council of magisters and command a slave guard of holy warriors armed with longaxes.
castle-evenfall-hall: The seat of House Tarth on the western coast of the island of Tarth, ancient home to a line that once ruled as kings before joining the realm of the Storm Kings through marriage. Its lords still bear the ancient title of Evenstar, and the castle's master-at-arms once trained a young Brienne of Tarth.
castle-parchments: The seat of House Penrose in the Stormlands, its exact location never given in the books.
city-braavos: The Secret City is the youngest and wealthiest of the nine Free Cities, founded by escaped slaves who fled the Valyrian Freehold to its farthest corner. Its diverse founders speak High Valyrian as a common tongue, and the city is home to the Faceless Men, an order of assassins who serve the Many-Faced God of death from the House of Black and White.
city-lorath: One of the nine Free Cities of Essos and the poorest and most isolated of them, built amid the ancient stone mazes of a vanished people. The Andal warlord Qarlon the Great once united its isles, but Valyrian dragonlords destroyed his army and razed Lorath itself in the Scouring of Lorath; the isles lay abandoned for over a century before being resettled by worshippers of the Blind God Boash.
city-qohor: The City of Sorcerers is the easternmost of the nine Free Cities, founded by Valyrian worshippers of the Black Goat on the edge of the forest that bears its name. The Black Goat demands daily blood sacrifice, and in times of great peril the city's high nobles are said to offer even their own children to placate the god.
ruin-ar-noy: A ruined Rhoynar city on the banks of the Qhoyne near its confluence with the Darkwash, once famed for its halls of green marble. It was destroyed by Valyrian dragons during the Rhoynish Wars, and the waters between its ruins and the Sorrows have since been left to pirates.
ruin-ny-sar: A ruined Rhoynar city at the confluence of the Rhoyne and the Noyne, once home to the palace of Princess Nymeria. It was destroyed during the Rhoynish Wars after Valyrian dragonlords crushed Prince Garin's army, prompting Nymeria to lead her surviving people away from Essos by ship; only the collapsed domes and broken spires of her marble palace remain.
ruin-the-sorrows-chroyane: A fog-shrouded stretch of the Rhoyne surrounding the ruined Rhoynar city of Chroyane, home to the Palace of Sorrow, once the largest palace in the known world. After Valyrian dragonlords crushed Prince Garin's army here, Garin is said to have cursed the river with water magic, unleashing a plague of greyscale that still afflicts the stone men who haunt the mists.
town-selhorys: A walled town on the Rhoyne under Volantene rule, larger than King's Landing or Oldtown despite its lesser status as a mere town. It was the site of Prince Garin's first victory over the Valyrian Freehold during the Second Spice War, a triumph that drew the full might of the dragonlords against the Rhoynar.
town-valysar: A walled Volantene town on the Rhoyne between Selhorys and Volon Therys, founded as a colony of the Valyrian Freehold. It was one of several towns won by Prince Garin during the Second Spice War before his ultimate defeat.
town-volon-therys: A walled Volantene town on the Rhoyne, facing the ruined Rhoynar city of Sar Mell across the water. Won by Prince Garin during the Second Spice War, it was flooded by Rhoynish water magic before falling back under Volantene rule.
ruin-sar-mell: A ruined Rhoynar city on the Rhoyne, once known for its flowers, that stands opposite Volon Therys. It was burned during the First Turtle War against that rival town and later sacked and its people enslaved in the final stage of the Rhoynish Wars against Valyria.
city-volantis: The oldest and proudest of the nine Free Cities, first colony of the Valyrian Freehold, its ancient quarter still enclosed within the towering Black Walls where only families of unbroken Valyrian descent may dwell. The city is ruled by triarchs elected from two rival factions, the war-favoring tigers and the trade-favoring elephants, a rivalry born from Volantis's failed bid to reclaim Valyria's old empire after the Doom.
city-mantarys: A Valyrian-founded city on the Sea of Sighs at the edge of the Lands of the Long Summer, one of several eastern colonies ruled directly by governors sent from Valyria rather than by self-rule. It survived the Doom of Valyria but has earned a sinister reputation as a city of monsters, its people said to be twisted and strange.
ruin-oros: A ruined city on the northern coast of the Smoking Sea in Essos, once said to rival Valyria itself in splendor before the Doom. Founded and governed directly by the Valyrian Freehold, its ruins remain inhabited by shadowy figures whose few tales are uniformly dark.
city-elyria: An island city in Slaver's Bay near the Valyrian peninsula, founded as a Valyrian colony ruled by governors sent from the Freehold rather than by self-rule. Though close enough to have been devastated, Elyria survived the Doom of Valyria intact.
city-tolos: A port city of central Essos famed for its slingers, who hurl soft lead balls capable of shattering flesh and bone. Founded under Valyrian rule, it forged ties with the Ghiscari slave cities of Slaver's Bay after the Doom and depends heavily on the slave trade.
ruin-ghozai: A ruined slaver port on the northern coast of the Isle of Cedars, its ancient brick walls and stepped pyramids drowned in a single night when the Doom of Valyria sent a wall of water three hundred feet high crashing over the island. Its broken pyramids are now home only to chattering monkeys.
ruin-velos: A ruined city on the southern tip of the Isle of Cedars, once known for its palaces of cedar and pink marble, destroyed along with the northern city of Ghozai by the tsunami unleashed by the Doom of Valyria. Only a handful of spearmen stationed on the island's highest hill survived to tell of its fall.
city-astapor: The Red City is the southernmost of the Ghiscari Slaver Cities on Slaver's Bay, a onetime colony of the Old Empire of Ghis that outlasted its fall and later thrived on the slave trade under Valyrian rule. It is famed above all for training the Unsullied, eunuch slave soldiers bred from childhood into disciplined, obedient legions.
city-yunkai: The Yellow City is one of the Ghiscari Slaver Cities on Slaver's Bay, built of yellow brick beneath crumbling towers and stepped pyramids and ruled by aristocrats known as the Wise Masters. Its main export is bed slaves, and like its rival cities it traces its harpy emblem back to the Old Empire of Ghis.
city-meereen: The largest of the Ghiscari Slaver Cities on Slaver's Bay, its nobility housed in stepped pyramids of colored brick crowned by the eight-hundred-foot Great Pyramid at the city's heart. Ruled for millennia by the slaving Great Masters rather than any king, the city traces its harpy emblem and pyramid architecture back to the Old Empire of Ghis.
city-vaes-dothrak: The City of Riders lies beneath the Mother of Mountains in the northeastern Dothraki sea, the only settlement of the nomadic Dothraki people. Home to the dosh khaleen, the widowed khaleesis who serve as the Dothraki's revered seers, the city forbids the drawing of blades or the shedding of free men's blood within its bounds.
ruin-old-ghis: The ruined heart of the ancient Old Empire of Ghis, once home to disciplined lockstep legions that rivaled any army in the known world. After five wars with the Valyrian Freehold, dragonfire finally razed the city and its pyramids to the ground, and the Valyrians sowed its fields with salt and sulfur to ensure it would never rise again.
city-new-ghis: The youngest, smallest, and most dynamic of the Ghiscari cities, an island city in the Gulf of Grief that claims to be Old Ghis reborn. Its iron legions, free men serving fixed terms rather than slaves, are trained and armed in the fashion of Astapor's Unsullied.
ruin-zamettar: A ruined city on the northern coast of Sothoryos, raised by the Ghiscari during the Old Empire before falling to Valyrian dragonlords during the Fourth Ghiscari War. Long abandoned, it was briefly resettled by Rhoynar fleeing Essos with Princess Nymeria, but disease and unexplained disappearances soon emptied it again.
ruin-yeen: A ruined city on the Zamoyos in Sothoryos, built entirely of massive blocks of oily black stone whose origin remains one of the great mysteries of the known world. Rhoynar fleeing with Princess Nymeria briefly settled there amid constant attacks from jungle ghouls, before its people vanished without explanation.
ruin-gogossos: Gogossos, once called Gorgai, is a ruined city on the Isle of Tears, founded by the Old Empire of Ghis before being captured and renamed by Valyrian dragonlords, who used it as a penal colony notorious for torture and blood magic. During the Century of Blood it grew wealthy on slavery and sorcery as the so-called Tenth Free City, until the Red Death plague emerged from its slave pens and left it abandoned.
castle-flints-finger: The seat of House Flint of Flint's Finger, a minor branch of the Flint family in the southwestern North. Once held by the Ironborn, it now swears fealty to House Bolton as Wardens of the North.
town-barrowton: A town in the North built around the Great Barrow, a great hill topped by Barrow Hall, the seat of House Dustin. Reputed to hold the grave of the First King of the First Men, the barrow gives the barrowlands their name, and the Dustins have ruled as bannermen of House Stark since the last Barrow King submitted at the end of the Thousand Years War.
castle-torrhens-square: The seat of House Tallhart, a stone-walled castle south of the wolfswood and north of Barrowton. Its lords are among the principal houses sworn to House Stark, bearing three green sentinel trees on a brown field.
ruin-bhorash: A ruined city on the northern coast of Slaver's Bay, south of the Painted Mountains, linked by old Valyrian roads to Mantarys and Tolos.
castle-pyke: The seat of House Greyjoy and the ruling castle of the Iron Islands, its towers and keeps standing on rocky sea stacks connected by swaying rope bridges as the cliff it was built on has eroded away. It has been held by the Greyjoys since Aegon's Conquest, and was heavily damaged during Balon Greyjoy's rebellion against the Iron Throne.
other-pebbleton: A fishing village on the eastern coast of Great Wyk in the Iron Islands, seat of House Merlyn. A square towerhouse with corner turrets overlooks the surrounding hovels, home to several thousand people who work the local fisheries.
castle-hammerhorn: The seat of the main line of House Goodbrother, a large square castle with spiked iron battlements set in the Hardstone Hills of southwestern Great Wyk. Unlike most Ironborn strongholds, its wealth comes not from the sea but from the mines beneath it.
ruin-naggas-ribs: Forty-four towering stone ribs on Nagga's Hill on Old Wyk, believed to be the remains of a sea dragon slain in the Age of Heroes. Legend holds that the Grey King raised his hall from Nagga's bones, and the site remains sacred ground where the Ironborn hold their kingsmoots to choose a king.
castle-ten-towers: The seat of House Harlaw on the isle of Harlaw, the richest and most populous of the Iron Islands. Built by the mercurial Lord Theomore Harlaw after he blamed the damp of the old Harlaw Hall for the deaths of his infant sons, it consists of ten dissimilar towers joined by wallwalks and covered bridges.
castle-faircastle: The seat of House Farman on Fair Isle off the Westerlands coast, an ancient First Men line that once ruled the island as petty kings and guarded the western coast from Ironborn raiders. The Farmans knelt to House Lannister generations before the Conquest, sealing the union through marriage to the last Farman king's daughter.
ruin-tyria: A ruined city across the Smoking Sea from the isle of Oros, founded as a Valyrian colony ruled by governors from the Freehold rather than by self-rule. Connected to the city of Valyria by an ancient road before the Doom, its ruins remain inhabited to some degree even now.
ruin-valyria: The capital of the Valyrian Freehold, the greatest civilization Essos had ever known, its dragonlords ruling much of the continent for five thousand years before the Doom of Valyria destroyed it in a single cataclysmic day. The disaster shattered the surrounding peninsula into fragments and birthed the volcanic Smoking Sea, leaving the city itself an ash-choked ruin that sailors still fear to approach.
city-qarth: The Queen of Cities is an ancient port on Essos's southern coast that controls the Jade Gates between the Summer and Jade Seas. It is contested by three rival powers, the Pureborn descendants of its ancient kings, the merchant princes known as the Thirteen, and the reclusive warlocks of the House of the Undying, each vying for dominance within its walls.
town-lord-hewetts-town: A large port town on the island of Oakenshield in the Reach, seat of House Hewett and twice the size of Lordsport on Pyke. It is one of the chief settlements of the Shield Islands, which guard the mouth of the Mander against raiders from the Iron Islands.
castle-casterly-rock: The seat of House Lannister, a stronghold carved into a great stone hill overlooking Lannisport and the Sunset Sea, said to resemble a lion in repose. According to legend it was won from the First Men house of Casterly by the trickster Lann the Clever, and the Lannisters ruled from it as Kings of the Rock until Aegon's Conquest reduced them to Lords Paramount of the West.
